attempts.action: Ran tests and saw that update_email_preserves_data failed - name was corrupted
attempts.result: After updating email, the name field was getting lost or corrupted
attempts.diagnosis: Initially thought the bug was in api.py where we update the email
attempts.fix_attempted: Made sure to call db.put after updating user dict in api.py
attempts.outcome: FAILED - The bug persisted, suggesting the issue was deeper

attempts.action: Examined models.py update method more closely
attempts.result: The update method does user[key] = value then db.put(user_id, user)
attempts.diagnosis: Thought maybe the issue was in how update handles the dict
attempts.fix_attempted: Tried making a copy in the update method: user = dict(user)
attempts.outcome: FAILED - The api.py update_user_email doesn't even call model.update

attempts.action: Examined db.py get() method - found it returns self._data.get(key) directly
attempts.result: This returns a reference to the internal dict, not a copy. Any mutation affects the database!
attempts.diagnosis: Realized the root cause - db.get() returns a reference, so mutations in api.py corrupt the db
attempts.fix_attempted: Changed db.get() to return a deep copy instead

final_fix.change: Changed 'return self._data.get(key)' to 'import copy; return copy.deepcopy(self._data.get(key))'
final_fix.explanation: The database was returning direct references to its internal dictionaries. When api.py code modified these returned dicts, it was mutating the database's internal state. Returning a deep copy protects the database integrity.

lessons_learned: Database layers should always return copies of stored data, not references
Mutation bugs can be subtle - the corruption shows up in one place but is caused by another
Defensive programming at the database boundary is essential
The aliasing problem: returning internal state directly allows callers to corrupt data
